model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With a fixed 256-step budget, the current run only covers a small fraction of the dataset. Increasing batch size should expose the model to substantially more examples within the same step budget, and a modest learning-rate increase keeps update scale reasonable after the batch-size change.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current model is still very shallow for CIFAR-10. Adding a third pooled convolutional stage should improve feature hierarchy and also shrink the flattened classifier input, making the architecture more convolution-heavy without adding much complexity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The deeper three-stage model is still quite narrow. A modest width increase, along with a slightly larger head, should raise representational capacity without changing the training loop or adding architectural complexity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)\n","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()\n","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The model now has substantially more capacity and is trained on only a partial pass through the data. Adding mild label smoothing is a low-complexity regularization change that can improve calibration and validation cross-entropy without increasing compute.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()\n","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)\n","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Label smoothing degraded validation loss on this workload. Reverting it restores the simpler loss setup before exploring other changes from the stronger pre-smoothing model state.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The architecture is now considerably stronger, and Adam's coupled weight decay may be suboptimal for it. Switching to AdamW with moderately stronger decoupled decay is a simple optimizer-family change that often improves validation loss on CNN image classifiers.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Under a fixed 256-step budget, moving from batch size 128 to 256 would let training cover roughly a full epoch and then some, which previously produced one of the strongest gains. A modest learning-rate increase keeps optimization speed aligned with the larger batch.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The biggest gains so far have come from using the fixed 256-step budget to cover more training examples. Increasing batch size again should push training closer to two full passes through the dataset, and a small learning-rate increase keeps the larger-batch updates from becoming too conservative.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        return 0.5 * (1.0 + np.cos(np.pi * progress))\n","old":"        return 0.1 + 0.9 * 0.5 * (1.0 + np.cos(np.pi * progress))\n","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"With the much larger batch size, training now covers nearly two epochs within the fixed 256-step budget. Letting the cosine schedule decay all the way to zero, instead of holding a 10% learning-rate floor, should improve late-budget consolidation without adding complexity or compute.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        return 0.1 + 0.9 * 0.5 * (1.0 + np.cos(np.pi * progress))\n","old":"        return 0.5 * (1.0 + np.cos(np.pi * progress))\n","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"Allowing the schedule to decay fully to zero hurt validation loss. Reverting to the previous 10% learning-rate floor restores the stronger scheduler before trying other directions.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The largest verified improvements have consistently come from spending the fixed 256-step budget on more examples. Pushing batch size to 512 should move training well past two full passes through the dataset, and a modest LR increase keeps optimization speed appropriate for the larger batch.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 640","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest pattern so far is that larger batches improve use of the fixed 256-step budget by exposing the model to more examples. A further increase to batch size 640, with a small matching LR bump, is the most direct continuation of the best-performing trend.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 640","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The verified trajectory still shows batch size as the dominant driver of improvement under the fixed 256-step budget. Increasing to batch size 768 continues that trend by covering even more examples per update cycle, with a modest LR increase to match the larger batch.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 896","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest verified trend remains increasing batch size to use the fixed 256-step budget on more examples. A smaller next step to batch size 896, paired with a modest learning-rate increase, continues that pattern while being less aggressive than a jump straight to 1024.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-3","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The batch-scaling trend appears close to saturating, suggesting optimization is no longer the only bottleneck. With larger effective data coverage and multiple passes through the dataset, slightly stronger decoupled weight decay is a simple regularization adjustment that may improve validation loss without changing compute.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Increasing weight decay degraded validation loss. Reverting to the previous 5e-4 setting restores the stronger optimizer configuration before using the remaining steps on other changes.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 4.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The move from batch size 768 to 896 produced only a marginal gain, which suggests the larger batch may benefit from a slightly less aggressive step size. Keeping the current best batch setting and backing off the learning rate is a simple way to test whether optimization is now over-shooting.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Lowering the learning rate from 4.5e-3 to 4e-3 clearly hurt validation loss. With one step remaining, the highest-value move is to restore the previously best-performing learning rate for the current batch and optimizer configuration.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}
